items.foco: Dermatophytes and keratin as a target
items.title: Dermatophytes and keratin
items.contentHtml:
Dermatophytes and keratin as a target

  • Dermatophytes (genera Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton) are keratinophilic fungi that infect the stratum corneum, hair and nails; keratin is their only source of nitrogen.
  • Invasion is limited to keratinized tissue because they lack the ability to penetrate beyond; The inflammatory response depends on the depth.
  • Clinical DDx: Ringworms vs. seborrheic dermatitis, psoriasis, eczema; KOH with 10-20% potassium hydroxide + Parker ink (or lactophenol blue) confirms septate hyaline hyphae.
  • Trap examination: dermatophytes do not invade mucous membranes or subcutaneous tissue; if there is visceral involvement, think about dimorphic fungi or immunosuppression.
  • Glossary: Keratin: insoluble structural protein that constitutes the metabolic target of dermatophytes; Its degradation by fungal keratinases allows colonization.

items.title: Tinea capitis and griseofulvin
items.contentHtml:
Tinea capitis and griseofulvin

  • Tinea capitis is the infection of the scalp by dermatophytes (Microsporum canis, Trichophyton tonsurans); It presents with scaly alopecic plaques + blackheads (fractured hair) or kerion (inflammatory mass).
  • First line: griseofulvin 10-20 mg/kg/day (max 500 mg) orally, for 6-8 weeks; It is the only antifungal that is deposited in keratin and does not require terbinafine in children <2 years.
  • Check trap: Topical antifungals do not penetrate the hair follicle, therefore they are ineffective; Treatment must always be systemic.
  • DDx: alopecia areata (without peeling or broken hair), folliculitis decalvans, trichotillomania; mycological culture confirms the agent.
  • Glossary: Kerion of Celsus: suppurative inflammatory form of tinea capitis, with follicular abscess; requires griseofulvin + topical corticosteroids to reduce scar.

items.title: Mucocutaneous candidiasis and fluconazole
items.contentHtml:
Mucocutaneous candidiasis and fluconazole

  • Mucocutaneous candidiasis (oral, vaginal, esophageal) caused by Candida albicans; risk factors: antibiotics, diabetes, immunosuppression, use of inhaled corticosteroids.
  • First line: fluconazole 150 mg single oral dose for vaginal candidiasis; 200-400 mg/day for 14-21 days for esophageal. Alternative: Topical nystatin or clotrimazole for mild forms.
  • Check test: Candida krusei is intrinsically resistant to fluconazole; C. glabratapresents dose-dependent resistance. If failure, use echinocandin (caspofungin) or amphotericin B.
  • DDx: EBV oral hairy leukoplakia (non-removable lesions), lichen planus, aphthous stomatitis; KOH shows pseudohyphae and blastoconidia.
  • Glossary: Pseudohypha: filamentous form of Candida with narrowing in the septa, indicative of active infection.

items.title: Meningeal cryptococcosis and amphotericin B
items.contentHtml:
Meningeal cryptococcosis and amphotericin B

  • Meningeal cryptococcosis due to Cryptococcus neoformans (HIV patients, transplant); presents with headache, fever, meningeal signs, intracranial hypertension.
  • First line (induction): liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day + fluconazole 400-800 mg/day for 2 weeks; then consolidation with fluconazole 400 mg/day for 8 weeks.
  • Trap examination: India ink in CSF shows capsule; cryptococcal antigen in serum/CSF is diagnostic (sensitivity >95%). Intracranial hypertension requires serial evacuative lumbar puncture.
  • DDx: tuberculous meningitis (lymphocytes, elevated ADA), cerebral toxoplasmosis (annular lesions on MRI).
  • Glossary: Amphotericin B: polyene antifungal that binds to ergosterol forming pores; Nephrotoxicity and electrolyte alterations are dose-limiting adverse effects.

items.title: Invasive aspergillosis and voriconazole
items.contentHtml:
Invasive aspergillosis and voriconazole

  • Invasive aspergillosis (mainly Aspergillus fumigatus) in neutropenics, transplant recipients, chronic use of corticosteroids; affects lung (infiltrates, nodules with halo sign) and dissemination.
  • First line: voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV every 12 hours day 1, then 4 mg/kg every 12 hours; or 200-300 mg every 12 hours orally. Alternative: liposomal amphotericin B if contraindicated.
  • Trap examination: the culture may be negative; serum galactomannan (index >0.5) and β-D-glucan are markers. Biopsy with dichotomous septate hyphae at a 45° angle confirms.
  • DDx: mucormycosis (non-septate hyphae, 90° angle), tuberculosis, Pneumocystis pneumonia.
  • Glossary: Halo sign: on CT, nodule surrounded by ground glass due to hemorrhage peripheral; suggestive of angioinvasive aspergillosis.

items.title: Mucormycosis and surgical debridement
items.contentHtml:
Mucormycosis and surgical debridement

  • Mucormycosis (Mucorales order: Rhizopus, Mucor) in patients with diabetic ketoacidosis, neutropenia, iron chelates; affects paranasal sinuses, brain, lung (necrotizing rhinitis, black eschar).
  • Essential treatment: aggressive surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue + liposomal amphotericin B 5-10 mg/kg/day; Debridement is mandatory because antifungals do not penetrate avascular areas.
  • Examination trap: voriconazole is ineffective (natural resistance); the biopsy shows wide, non-septate hyphae, with branching at a right angle (90°).
  • DDx: invasive aspergillosis (septate hyphae, 45° angle), bacterial sinusitis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
  • Glossary: Eschar black: ischemic necrotic tissue on the palate or nose, pathognomonic of rhinocerebral mucormycosis.

items.title: Penicillins: transpeptidase
items.contentHtml:
Penicillins inhibit transpeptidase

  • Penicillins are β-lactam antibiotics that inhibit transpeptidase (PBP, penicillin-binding protein), an enzyme that catalyzes the cross-linking of peptidoglycans in the bacterial wall, blocking synthesis and causing osmotic lysis.
  • Spectrum: penicillin G (gram+ cocci, spirochetes), amoxicillin (same + H. influenzae), ampicillin (covers enterococci and Listeria).
  • Check trap: penicillins are bactericidal only in the growth phase; not active against wallless bacteria (Mycoplasma) or L forms. Resistance by β-lactamases (S. aureus) is overcome with clavulanic acid.
  • Allergy DDx: maculopapular rash (frequent) vs. anaphylaxis (rare); penicilloyl-polylysine skin test if suspected.
  • Glossary: PBP: penicillin-binding membrane proteins; Its alteration (e.g. MRSA) confers resistance.

items.title: Vancomycin and enterococcus resistance
items.contentHtml:
Vancomycin and resistance in enterococcus

  • Vancomycin is a glycopeptide that inhibits peptidoglycan polymerization by binding to the D-Ala-D-Ala end; active against gram+ (MRSA, Clostridium difficile).
  • Resistance in enterococcus (VRE): mediated by vanA (high resistance) or vanB (variable resistance) operon, which modify the target to D-Ala-D-Lac, reducing affinity 1000 times.
  • Examination trap: VRE (E. faecium most common) requires linezolid or daptomycin; vancomycin should not be used. Monitoring trough levels (10-15 mg/L; 15-20 for pneumonia) avoids nephrotoxicity.
  • DDx: sensitive enterococcus infection vs. resistant; the antibiogram with E-test or microdilution is key.
  • Glossary: VRE: Enterococcus faecium/fecalis resistant to vancomycin; risk factor: prolonged hospitalization, previous use of vancomycin.

items.title: 3rd generation cephalosporins
items.contentHtml:
Third generation cephalosporins

  • Third generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, ceftazidime) have greater activity against gram-negative bacteria (enterobacteria, Neisseria, H. influenzae) and penetrate CSF, unlike the 1st and 2nd generation.
  • Indications key: Bacterial meningitis (ceftriaxone 2 g IV every 12 hours + vancomycin), community-acquired pneumonia, gonorrhea, abdominal sepsis. Exam trap: Ceftriaxone has biliary excretion (pseudolithiasis) and does not cover Listeria or Pseudomonas (except ceftazidime). Extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBL) resistance inactivates all cephalosporins; use carbapenems.
  • DDx: 4th generation cephalosporins (cefepime) also cover Pseudomonas and have greater activity against gram+.
  • Glossary: ESBL: enzymes that hydrolyze 3rd generation cephalosporins and aztreonam; encoded in plasmids.

items.title: Meropenem in bacterial meningitis
items.contentHtml:
Meropenem in bacterial meningitis

  • Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem (gram+, gram-, anaerobes) that penetrates well into the CSF in inflamed meninges; indicated in severe bacterial meningitis, especially due to multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacilli or when cephalosporins fail.
  • Dose: 2 g IV every 8 hours (prolonged infusion 3 hours); in meningitis, duration 10-14 days depending on germ and evolution.
  • Examination trap: meropenem is the carbapenem of choice in meningitis because imipenem/cilastatin has a risk of seizures. Does not cover MRSA or resistant Enterococcus faecium.
  • DDx: Listeria meningitis requires ampicillin; for Neisseria meningitidis, ceftriaxone is first line. Meropenem is reserved for nosocomial or ESBL meningitis.
  • Glossary: Carbapenem: Broad-spectrum β-lactam with resistance to most β-lactamases, except metallo-β-lactamases (NDM).
